features.summary: Bank of America reported Q1 2022 net income of $7.1 billion ($0.80 per share) with strong organic growth across all business segments. Net interest income grew 13% year-over-year to $11.7 billion, driven by deposit growth and loan expansion. The bank achieved its third consecutive quarter of operating leverage with revenue growth outpacing expense management. Commercial loans grew $13 billion excluding PPP runoff, while consumer spending remained robust with deposit balances 47% above pre-pandemic levels.
features.management_outlook: Management expects significant NII improvement through 2022, projecting Q2 NII to increase by more than $650 million from Q1 levels, with continued sequential growth in following quarters. The bank anticipates flat expenses for 2022 versus 2021, targeting gradual improvement in efficiency ratio toward 60%. Asset sensitivity of $5.4 billion provides substantial upside from rising rates, with 90% driven by short-rate movements. Management remains confident in continued loan growth and deposit stability based on strong customer fundamentals.
features.risks: Key risks include potential economic slowdown from aggressive Fed tightening, geopolitical impacts from Russia-Ukraine conflict, and rising inflation pressures. The bank has $700 million direct exposure to nine Russian borrowers, now classified as criticized. AOCI volatility from rising rates reduced CET1 ratio by 21 basis points this quarter, though management views this as temporary. Credit normalization is expected as pandemic-era support measures fade, though current asset quality metrics remain strong.
features.key_drivers: Primary earnings drivers include substantial NII growth from rising interest rates given $2 trillion deposit base with significant zero-cost checking accounts. Loan growth momentum continues with commercial utilization rates recovering toward normal levels and consumer lending showing green shoots. Digital adoption accelerated with over 50% digital sales penetration and Zelle/Erica usage up 4x from pre-pandemic levels. The bank's G-SIB buffer will increase 50 basis points by 2024, requiring gradual capital build toward 10.75%-11% CET1 target range.
